Height 7.5m | Burial Facility = Double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} (Takakurayama-style chamber) | Artifacts = Gold rings, beads, weapons, horse fittings, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} | Subsidiary Tumuli = | Construction Period = Late 6th century | Occupant = (Possibly) a chief of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} | Mausoleum = | Historical Site = Ise City Designated Historical Site "Takakurayama Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Properties = | Notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Icon = 円墳 | Label = Takakurayama Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Takakurayama Kofun''' (高倉山古墳) is a burial mound located in Toyokawa-cho, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the sacred precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). The mound is designated as an Ise City Historical Site. The kofun is renowned for housing one of Japan's largest {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}}, but entry is restricted as it lies within the precincts of Toyouke Daijingu. == Overview == [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 02.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Takakurayama, where the kofun is located}}]] The kofun is situated atop {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} (elevation 116 meters), within the sacred precincts of the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The mound has been mentioned in historical documents dating back to the Kamakura period and was widely venerated during the Edo period as a {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}} (Heavenly Rock Cave). This veneration led to significant alterations to the mound due to erosion and flattening. Surveys and excavations were conducted in 1975. The tumulus is believed to have been circular, with an estimated diameter of over 35 meters (possibly over 40 meters) and a height of approximately 7.5 meters (or 8.5 meters). No evidence of stone coverings or haniwa has been found on the surface. The burial facility is a double-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the southwest. With a total length of 18.5 meters, it is the largest stone chamber in the Ise region and ranks sixth nationwide in length and second in floor area of the main chamber. Artifacts include gold rings, beads, horse fittings, swords, and pottery. The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. Designated December 5, 1973. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == References == {{small|Sources used in this article:}} * Publications by local governments: * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2005|chapter=Takakurayama Kofun|title=History of Mie Prefecture: Archaeological Materials 1|publisher=Mie Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Mie History)|2005}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2011|chapter=Special Topics > Takakurayama Kofun|title=Ise City History Volume 6: Archaeology|publisher=Ise City|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise History)|2011}}}} == Related Literature == {{small|Additional references not directly cited in this article:}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=1965|chapter=Ise City’s Takakurayama Burial Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Round Barrows]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai clan|Burial Mound]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:New pages]][[category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)]]

Height 7.5m | Burial Facility = Double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} (Takakurayama-style chamber) | Artifacts = Gold rings, beads, weapons, horse fittings, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} | Subsidiary Tumuli = | Construction Period = Late 6th century | Occupant = (Possibly) a chief of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} | Mausoleum = | Historical Site = Ise City Designated Historical Site "Takakurayama Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Properties = | Notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Icon = 円墳 | Label = Takakurayama Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Takakurayama Kofun''' (高倉山古墳) is a burial mound located in Toyokawa-cho, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the sacred precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). The mound is designated as an Ise City Historical Site. The kofun is renowned for housing one of Japan's largest {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}}, but entry is restricted as it lies within the precincts of Toyouke Daijingu. == Overview == [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 02.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Takakurayama, where the kofun is located}}]] The kofun is situated atop {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} (elevation 116 meters), within the sacred precincts of the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The mound has been mentioned in historical documents dating back to the Kamakura period and was widely venerated during the Edo period as a {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}} (Heavenly Rock Cave). This veneration led to significant alterations to the mound due to erosion and flattening. Surveys and excavations were conducted in 1975. The tumulus is believed to have been circular, with an estimated diameter of over 35 meters (possibly over 40 meters) and a height of approximately 7.5 meters (or 8.5 meters). No evidence of stone coverings or haniwa has been found on the surface. The burial facility is a double-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the southwest. With a total length of 18.5 meters, it is the largest stone chamber in the Ise region and ranks sixth nationwide in length and second in floor area of the main chamber. Artifacts include gold rings, beads, horse fittings, swords, and pottery. The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. Designated December 5, 1973. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == References == {{small|Sources used in this article:}} * Publications by local governments: * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2005|chapter=Takakurayama Kofun|title=History of Mie Prefecture: Archaeological Materials 1|publisher=Mie Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Mie History)|2005}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2011|chapter=Special Topics > Takakurayama Kofun|title=Ise City History Volume 6: Archaeology|publisher=Ise City|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise History)|2011}}}} == Related Literature == {{small|Additional references not directly cited in this article:}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=1965|chapter=Ise City’s Takakurayama Burial Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Round Barrows]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai clan|Burial Mound]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[category:original pages]] [[category:original pages]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)]]

[[Category:pp]][[category:Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)]][[Category:pp]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]]

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Takakurayama Kofun | image = [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 03.JPG|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber (1978) | Alternate name = | Cluster = | location = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Toyokawa-cho
(within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}}) | Latitude = 34|28|55.70 | Longitude = 136|41|56.83 | ISO = JP-24 | Type = {{ill|Round barrow|ja|円墳}} | Size = Diameter over 35m
Height 7.5m | Burial Facility = Double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} (Takakurayama-style chamber) | Artifacts = Gold rings, beads, weapons, horse fittings, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} | Subsidiary Tumuli = | Construction Period = Late 6th century | Occupant = (Possibly) a chief of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} | Mausoleum = | Historical Site = Ise City Designated Historical Site "Takakurayama Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Properties = | Notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Icon = 円墳 | Label = Takakurayama Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Takakurayama Kofun''' (高倉山古墳) is a burial mound located in Toyokawa-cho, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the sacred precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). The mound is designated as an Ise City Historical Site. The kofun is renowned for housing one of Japan's largest {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}}, but entry is restricted as it lies within the precincts of Toyouke Daijingu. == Overview == [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 02.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Takakurayama, where the kofun is located}}]] The kofun is situated atop {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} (elevation 116 meters), within the sacred precincts of the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The mound has been mentioned in historical documents dating back to the Kamakura period and was widely venerated during the Edo period as a {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}} (Heavenly Rock Cave). This veneration led to significant alterations to the mound due to erosion and flattening. Surveys and excavations were conducted in 1975. The tumulus is believed to have been circular, with an estimated diameter of over 35 meters (possibly over 40 meters) and a height of approximately 7.5 meters (or 8.5 meters). No evidence of stone coverings or haniwa has been found on the surface. The burial facility is a double-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the southwest. With a total length of 18.5 meters, it is the largest stone chamber in the Ise region and ranks sixth nationwide in length and second in floor area of the main chamber. Artifacts include gold rings, beads, horse fittings, swords, and pottery. The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. Designated December 5, 1973. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == References == {{small|Sources used in this article:}} * Publications by local governments: * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2005|chapter=Takakurayama Kofun|title=History of Mie Prefecture: Archaeological Materials 1|publisher=Mie Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Mie History)|2005}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2011|chapter=Special Topics > Takakurayama Kofun|title=Ise City History Volume 6: Archaeology|publisher=Ise City|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise History)|2011}}}} == Related Literature == {{small|Additional references not directly cited in this article:}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=1965|chapter=Ise City’s Takakurayama Burial Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Round Barrows]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai clan|Burial Mound]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:pp]] [[category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[ja:高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)]]

Designated December 5, 1973. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == References == {{small|Sources used in this article:}} * Publications by local governments: * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2005|chapter=Takakurayama Kofun|title=History of Mie Prefecture: Archaeological Materials 1|publisher=Mie Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Mie History)|2005}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2011|chapter=Special Topics > Takakurayama Kofun|title=Ise City History Volume 6: Archaeology|publisher=Ise City|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise History)|2011}}}} == Related Literature == {{small|Additional references not directly cited in this article:}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=1965|chapter=Ise City’s Takakurayama Burial Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Round Barrows]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai clan|Burial Mound]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:pp]] [[category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)]]

#REDIRECT [[Takakurayama Burial Mound]]
[[Category:pp]][[category:Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)]][[Category:pp]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]]

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Takakurayama Kofun | image = [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 03.JPG|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber (1978) | Alternate name = | Cluster = | location = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Toyokawa-cho
(within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}}) | Latitude = 34|28|55.70 | Longitude = 136|41|56.83 | ISO = JP-24 | Type = {{ill|Round barrow|ja|円墳}} | Size = Diameter over 35m
Height 7.5m | Burial Facility = Double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} (Takakurayama-style chamber) | Artifacts = Gold rings, beads, weapons, horse fittings, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} | Subsidiary Tumuli = | Construction Period = Late 6th century | Occupant = (Possibly) a chief of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} | Mausoleum = | Historical Site = Ise City Designated Historical Site "Takakurayama Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Properties = | Notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Icon = 円墳 | Label = Takakurayama Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Takakurayama Kofun''' (高倉山古墳) is a burial mound located in Toyokawa-cho, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the sacred precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). The mound is designated as an Ise City Historical Site. The kofun is renowned for housing one of Japan's largest {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}}, but entry is restricted as it lies within the precincts of Toyouke Daijingu. == Overview == [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 02.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Takakurayama, where the kofun is located}}]] The kofun is situated atop {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} (elevation 116 meters), within the sacred precincts of the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The mound has been mentioned in historical documents dating back to the Kamakura period and was widely venerated during the Edo period as a {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}} (Heavenly Rock Cave). This veneration led to significant alterations to the mound due to erosion and flattening. Surveys and excavations were conducted in 1975. The tumulus is believed to have been circular, with an estimated diameter of over 35 meters (possibly over 40 meters) and a height of approximately 7.5 meters (or 8.5 meters). No evidence of stone coverings or haniwa has been found on the surface. The burial facility is a double-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the southwest. With a total length of 18.5 meters, it is the largest stone chamber in the Ise region and ranks sixth nationwide in length and second in floor area of the main chamber. Artifacts include gold rings, beads, horse fittings, swords, and pottery. The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. Designated December 5, 1973. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == References == {{small|Sources used in this article:}} * Publications by local governments: * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2005|chapter=Takakurayama Kofun|title=History of Mie Prefecture: Archaeological Materials 1|publisher=Mie Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Mie History)|2005}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2011|chapter=Special Topics > Takakurayama Kofun|title=Ise City History Volume 6: Archaeology|publisher=Ise City|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise History)|2011}}}} == Related Literature == {{small|Additional references not directly cited in this article:}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=1965|chapter=Ise City’s Takakurayama Burial Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Round Barrows]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai clan|Burial Mound]] [[category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]] [[category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)]]

The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. Designated December 5, 1973. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == References == {{small|Sources used in this article:}} * Publications by local governments: * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2005|chapter=Takakurayama Kofun|title=History of Mie Prefecture: Archaeological Materials 1|publisher=Mie Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Mie History)|2005}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2011|chapter=Special Topics > Takakurayama Kofun|title=Ise City History Volume 6: Archaeology|publisher=Ise City|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise History)|2011}}}} == Related Literature == {{small|Additional references not directly cited in this article:}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=1965|chapter=Ise City’s Takakurayama Burial Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} [[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Watarai clan|Burial Mound]] [[category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]] [[category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[ja:高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)]]

The mound is designated as an Ise City Historical Site. The kofun is renowned for housing one of Japan's largest {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}}, but entry is restricted as it lies within the precincts of Toyouke Daijingu. == Overview == [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 02.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Takakurayama, where the kofun is located}}]] The kofun is situated atop {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} (elevation 116 meters), within the sacred precincts of the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The mound has been mentioned in historical documents dating back to the Kamakura period and was widely venerated during the Edo period as a {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}} (Heavenly Rock Cave). This veneration led to significant alterations to the mound due to erosion and flattening. Surveys and excavations were conducted in 1975. The tumulus is believed to have been circular, with an estimated diameter of over 35 meters (possibly over 40 meters) and a height of approximately 7.5 meters (or 8.5 meters). No evidence of stone coverings or haniwa has been found on the surface. The burial facility is a double-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the southwest. With a total length of 18.5 meters, it is the largest stone chamber in the Ise region and ranks sixth nationwide in length and second in floor area of the main chamber. Artifacts include gold rings, beads, horse fittings, swords, and pottery. The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai {{Infobox ancient site | name = Takakurayama Kofun | image = [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 03.JPG|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber (1978) | Alternate name = | Cluster = | location = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Toyokawa-cho
(within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}}) | Latitude = 34|28|55.70 | Longitude = 136|41|56.83 | ISO = JP-24 | Type = {{ill|Round barrow|ja|円墳}} | Size = Diameter over 35m
Height 7.5m | Burial Facility = Double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} (Takakurayama-style chamber) | Artifacts = Gold rings, beads, weapons, horse fittings, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} | Subsidiary Tumuli = | Construction Period = Late 6th century | Occupant = (Possibly) a chief of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} | Mausoleum = | Historical Site = Ise City Designated Historical Site "Takakurayama Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Properties = | Notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Icon = 円墳 | Label = Takakurayama Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Takakurayama Kofun''' (高倉山古墳) is a burial mound located in Toyokawa-cho, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the sacred precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). The mound is designated as an Ise City Historical Site. The kofun is renowned for housing one of Japan's largest {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}}, but entry is restricted as it lies within the precincts of Toyouke Daijingu. == Overview == [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 02.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Takakurayama, where the kofun is located}}]] The kofun is situated atop {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} (elevation 116 meters), within the sacred precincts of the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The mound has been mentioned in historical documents dating back to the Kamakura period and was widely venerated during the Edo period as a {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}} (Heavenly Rock Cave). This veneration led to significant alterations to the mound due to erosion and flattening. Surveys and excavations were conducted in 1975. The tumulus is believed to have been circular, with an estimated diameter of over 35 meters (possibly over 40 meters) and a height of approximately 7.5 meters (or 8.5 meters). No evidence of stone coverings or haniwa has been found on the surface. The burial facility is a double-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the southwest. With a total length of 18.5 meters, it is the largest stone chamber in the Ise region and ranks sixth nationwide in length and second in floor area of the main chamber. Artifacts include gold rings, beads, horse fittings, swords, and pottery. The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. Designated December 5, 1973. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == References == {{small|Sources used in this article:}} * Publications by local governments: * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2005|chapter=Takakurayama Kofun|title=History of Mie Prefecture: Archaeological Materials 1|publisher=Mie Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Mie History)|2005}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2011|chapter=Special Topics > Takakurayama Kofun|title=Ise City History Volume 6: Archaeology|publisher=Ise City|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise History)|2011}}}} == Related Literature == {{small|Additional references not directly cited in this article:}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=1965|chapter=Ise City’s Takakurayama Burial Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] Mound]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]] {{Kofun navbar}}[[Category:kk]] [[ja:高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)]]

{{Infobox ancient site | name = Takakurayama Kofun | image = [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 03.JPG|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber (1978) | Alternate name = | Cluster = | location = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Toyokawa-cho
(within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}}) | Latitude = 34|28|55.70 | Longitude = 136|41|56.83 | ISO = JP-24 | Type = {{ill|Round barrow|ja|円墳}} | Size = Diameter over 35m
Height 7.5m | Burial Facility = Double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} (Takakurayama-style chamber) | Artifacts = Gold rings, beads, weapons, horse fittings, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} | Subsidiary Tumuli = | Construction Period = Late 6th century | Occupant = (Possibly) a chief of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} | Mausoleum = | Historical Site = Ise City Designated Historical Site "Takakurayama Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Properties = | Notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Icon = 円墳 | Label = Takakurayama Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Takakurayama Kofun''' (高倉山古墳) is a burial mound located in Toyokawa-cho, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the sacred precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). The mound is designated as an Ise City Historical Site. The kofun is renowned for housing one of Japan's largest {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}}, but entry is restricted as it lies within the precincts of Toyouke Daijingu. == Overview == [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 02.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Takakurayama, where the kofun is located}}]] The kofun is situated atop {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} (elevation 116 meters), within the sacred precincts of the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The mound has been mentioned in historical documents dating back to the Kamakura period and was widely venerated during the Edo period as a {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}} (Heavenly Rock Cave). This veneration led to significant alterations to the mound due to erosion and flattening. Surveys and excavations were conducted in 1975. The tumulus is believed to have been circular, with an estimated diameter of over 35 meters (possibly over 40 meters) and a height of approximately 7.5 meters (or 8.5 meters). No evidence of stone coverings or haniwa has been found on the surface. The burial facility is a double-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the southwest. With a total length of 18.5 meters, it is the largest stone chamber in the Ise region and ranks sixth nationwide in length and second in floor area of the main chamber. Artifacts include gold rings, beads, horse fittings, swords, and pottery. The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. Designated December 5, 1973. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == References == {{small|Sources used in this article:}} * Publications by local governments: * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2005|chapter=Takakurayama Kofun|title=History of Mie Prefecture: Archaeological Materials 1|publisher=Mie Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Mie History)|2005}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2011|chapter=Special Topics > Takakurayama Kofun|title=Ise City History Volume 6: Archaeology|publisher=Ise City|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise History)|2011}}}} == Related Literature == {{small|Additional references not directly cited in this article:}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=1965|chapter=Ise City’s Takakurayama Burial Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Municipal Historic Sites in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Watarai clan|Burial Mound]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Takakurayama Burial Mound]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:kk]] [[ja:高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)]]

The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. {{Infobox ancient site | name = Takakurayama Kofun | image = [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 03.JPG|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber (1978) | Alternate name = | Cluster = | location = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Toyokawa-cho
(within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}}) | Latitude = 34|28|55.70 | Longitude = 136|41|56.83 | ISO = JP-24 | Type = {{ill|Round barrow|ja|円墳}} | Size = Diameter over 35m
Height 7.5m | Burial Facility = Double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} (Takakurayama-style chamber) | Artifacts = Gold rings, beads, weapons, horse fittings, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} | Subsidiary Tumuli = | Construction Period = Late 6th century | Occupant = (Possibly) a chief of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} | Mausoleum = | Historical Site = Ise City Designated Historical Site "Takakurayama Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Properties = | Notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Icon = 円墳 | Label = Takakurayama Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Takakurayama Kofun''' (高倉山古墳) is a burial mound located in Toyokawa-cho, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the sacred precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). The mound is designated as an Ise City Historical Site. The kofun is renowned for housing one of Japan's largest {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}}, but entry is restricted as it lies within the precincts of Toyouke Daijingu. == Overview == [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 02.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Takakurayama, where the kofun is located}}]] The kofun is situated atop {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} (elevation 116 meters), within the sacred precincts of the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The mound has been mentioned in historical documents dating back to the Kamakura period and was widely venerated during the Edo period as a {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}} (Heavenly Rock Cave). This veneration led to significant alterations to the mound due to erosion and flattening. Surveys and excavations were conducted in 1975. The tumulus is believed to have been circular, with an estimated diameter of over 35 meters (possibly over 40 meters) and a height of approximately 7.5 meters (or 8.5 meters). No evidence of stone coverings or haniwa has been found on the surface. The burial facility is a double-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the southwest. With a total length of 18.5 meters, it is the largest stone chamber in the Ise region and ranks sixth nationwide in length and second in floor area of the main chamber. Artifacts include gold rings, beads, horse fittings, swords, and pottery. The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. Designated December 5, 1973. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == References == {{small|Sources used in this article:}} * Publications by local governments: * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2005|chapter=Takakurayama Kofun|title=History of Mie Prefecture: Archaeological Materials 1|publisher=Mie Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Mie History)|2005}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2011|chapter=Special Topics > Takakurayama Kofun|title=Ise City History Volume 6: Archaeology|publisher=Ise City|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise History)|2011}}}} == Related Literature == {{small|Additional references not directly cited in this article:}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=1965|chapter=Ise City’s Takakurayama Burial Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] {{Infobox ancient site | name = Takakurayama Kofun | image = [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 03.JPG|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber (1978) | Alternate name = | Cluster = | location = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Toyokawa-cho
(within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}}) | Latitude = 34|28|55.70 | Longitude = 136|41|56.83 | ISO = JP-24 | Type = {{ill|Round barrow|ja|円墳}} | Size = Diameter over 35m
Height 7.5m | Burial Facility = Double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} (Takakurayama-style chamber) | Artifacts = Gold rings, beads, weapons, horse fittings, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} | Subsidiary Tumuli = | Construction Period = Late 6th century | Occupant = (Possibly) a chief of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} | Mausoleum = | Historical Site = Ise City Designated Historical Site "Takakurayama Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Properties = | Notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Icon = 円墳 | Label = Takakurayama Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Takakurayama Kofun''' (高倉山古墳) is a burial mound located in Toyokawa-cho, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the sacred precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). The mound is designated as an Ise City Historical Site. The kofun is renowned for housing one of Japan's largest {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}}, but entry is restricted as it lies within the precincts of Toyouke Daijingu. == Overview == [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 02.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Takakurayama, where the kofun is located}}]] The kofun is situated atop {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} (elevation 116 meters), within the sacred precincts of the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The mound has been mentioned in historical documents dating back to the Kamakura period and was widely venerated during the Edo period as a {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}} (Heavenly Rock Cave). This veneration led to significant alterations to the mound due to erosion and flattening. Surveys and excavations were conducted in 1975. The tumulus is believed to have been circular, with an estimated diameter of over 35 meters (possibly over 40 meters) and a height of approximately 7.5 meters (or 8.5 meters). No evidence of stone coverings or haniwa has been found on the surface. The burial facility is a double-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the southwest. With a total length of 18.5 meters, it is the largest stone chamber in the Ise region and ranks sixth nationwide in length and second in floor area of the main chamber. Artifacts include gold rings, beads, horse fittings, swords, and pottery. The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. Designated December 5, 1973. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == References == {{small|Sources used in this article:}} * Publications by local governments: * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2005|chapter=Takakurayama Kofun|title=History of Mie Prefecture: Archaeological Materials 1|publisher=Mie Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Mie History)|2005}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2011|chapter=Special Topics > Takakurayama Kofun|title=Ise City History Volume 6: Archaeology|publisher=Ise City|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise History)|2011}}}} == Related Literature == {{small|Additional references not directly cited in this article:}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=1965|chapter=Ise City’s Takakurayama Burial Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. Height 7.5m | Burial Facility = Double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} (Takakurayama-style chamber) | Artifacts = Gold rings, beads, weapons, horse fittings, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} | Subsidiary Tumuli = | Construction Period = Late 6th century | Occupant = (Possibly) a chief of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} | Mausoleum = | Historical Site = Ise City Designated Historical Site "Takakurayama Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Properties = | Notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Icon = 円墳 | Label = Takakurayama Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Takakurayama Kofun''' (高倉山古墳) is a burial mound located in Toyokawa-cho, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the sacred precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). The mound is designated as an Ise City Historical Site. The kofun is renowned for housing one of Japan's largest {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}}, but entry is restricted as it lies within the precincts of Toyouke Daijingu. == Overview == [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 02.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Takakurayama, where the kofun is located}}]] The kofun is situated atop {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} (elevation 116 meters), within the sacred precincts of the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The mound has been mentioned in historical documents dating back to the Kamakura period and was widely venerated during the Edo period as a {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}} (Heavenly Rock Cave). This veneration led to significant alterations to the mound due to erosion and flattening. Surveys and excavations were conducted in 1975. The tumulus is believed to have been circular, with an estimated diameter of over 35 meters (possibly over 40 meters) and a height of approximately 7.5 meters (or 8.5 meters). No evidence of stone coverings or haniwa has been found on the surface. The burial facility is a double-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the southwest. With a total length of 18.5 meters, it is the largest stone chamber in the Ise region and ranks sixth nationwide in length and second in floor area of the main chamber. Artifacts include gold rings, beads, horse fittings, swords, and pottery. The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. {{Infobox ancient site | name = Takakurayama Kofun | image = [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 03.JPG|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber (1978) | Alternate name = | Cluster = | location = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Toyokawa-cho
(within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}}) | Latitude = 34|28|55.70 | Longitude = 136|41|56.83 | ISO = JP-24 | Type = {{ill|Round barrow|ja|円墳}} | Size = Diameter over 35m
Height 7.5m | Burial Facility = Double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} (Takakurayama-style chamber) | Artifacts = Gold rings, beads, weapons, horse fittings, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} | Subsidiary Tumuli = | Construction Period = Late 6th century | Occupant = (Possibly) a chief of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} | Mausoleum = | Historical Site = Ise City Designated Historical Site "Takakurayama Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Properties = | Notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Icon = 円墳 | Label = Takakurayama Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Takakurayama Kofun''' (高倉山古墳) is a burial mound located in Toyokawa-cho, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the sacred precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). The mound is designated as an Ise City Historical Site. The kofun is renowned for housing one of Japan's largest {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}}, but entry is restricted as it lies within the precincts of Toyouke Daijingu. == Overview == [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 02.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Takakurayama, where the kofun is located}}]] The kofun is situated atop {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} (elevation 116 meters), within the sacred precincts of the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The mound has been mentioned in historical documents dating back to the Kamakura period and was widely venerated during the Edo period as a {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}} (Heavenly Rock Cave). This veneration led to significant alterations to the mound due to erosion and flattening. Surveys and excavations were conducted in 1975. The tumulus is believed to have been circular, with an estimated diameter of over 35 meters (possibly over 40 meters) and a height of approximately 7.5 meters (or 8.5 meters). No evidence of stone coverings or haniwa has been found on the surface. The burial facility is a double-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the southwest. With a total length of 18.5 meters, it is the largest stone chamber in the Ise region and ranks sixth nationwide in length and second in floor area of the main chamber. Artifacts include gold rings, beads, horse fittings, swords, and pottery. The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. Designated December 5, 1973. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == References == {{small|Sources used in this article:}} * Publications by local governments: * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2005|chapter=Takakurayama Kofun|title=History of Mie Prefecture: Archaeological Materials 1|publisher=Mie Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Mie History)|2005}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2011|chapter=Special Topics > Takakurayama Kofun|title=Ise City History Volume 6: Archaeology|publisher=Ise City|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise History)|2011}}}} == Related Literature == {{small|Additional references not directly cited in this article:}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=1965|chapter=Ise City’s Takakurayama Burial Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site | name = Takakurayama Kofun | image = [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 03.JPG|280px]]
Entrance to the stone chamber (1978) | Alternate name = | Cluster = | location = {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, Toyokawa-cho
(within the precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}}) | Latitude = 34|28|55.70 | Longitude = 136|41|56.83 | ISO = JP-24 | Type = {{ill|Round barrow|ja|円墳}} | Size = Diameter over 35m
Height 7.5m | Burial Facility = Double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}} (Takakurayama-style chamber) | Artifacts = Gold rings, beads, weapons, horse fittings, {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}}, {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} | Subsidiary Tumuli = | Construction Period = Late 6th century | Occupant = (Possibly) a chief of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} | Mausoleum = | Historical Site = Ise City Designated Historical Site "Takakurayama Kofun" | Tangible Cultural Properties = | Notes = | Map = Japan Mie | Icon = 円墳 | Label = Takakurayama Kofun | Label Position = top }} '''Takakurayama Kofun''' (高倉山古墳) is a burial mound located in Toyokawa-cho, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the sacred precincts of {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}). The mound is designated as an Ise City Historical Site. The kofun is renowned for housing one of Japan's largest {{ill|corridor-style stone chambers|ja|横穴式石室}}, but entry is restricted as it lies within the precincts of Toyouke Daijingu. == Overview == [[File:Takakura-yama(Ise) 02.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Takakurayama, where the kofun is located}}]] The kofun is situated atop {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} (elevation 116 meters), within the sacred precincts of the Outer Shrine of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The mound has been mentioned in historical documents dating back to the Kamakura period and was widely venerated during the Edo period as a {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}} (Heavenly Rock Cave). This veneration led to significant alterations to the mound due to erosion and flattening. Surveys and excavations were conducted in 1975. The tumulus is believed to have been circular, with an estimated diameter of over 35 meters (possibly over 40 meters) and a height of approximately 7.5 meters (or 8.5 meters). No evidence of stone coverings or haniwa has been found on the surface. The burial facility is a double-sleeve corridor-style stone chamber, opening to the southwest. With a total length of 18.5 meters, it is the largest stone chamber in the Ise region and ranks sixth nationwide in length and second in floor area of the main chamber. Artifacts include gold rings, beads, horse fittings, swords, and pottery. The mound is believed to have been constructed in the late 6th century, with additional burials continuing until the early 7th century. == Excavation History == * '''Kamakura period''': Mentioned in the ''Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki'', ''Tenchi Reikiki'', and ''Kokuzo-no-hisho'', referring to a stone cave on Takakurayama. * '''Muromachi period''': The ''Jingi Hisho'' records the dimensions of the stone chamber. * '''Edo period''': Venerated as {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天岩戸}}, with a kagura hall built on the northern slope of the mound and tea shops nearby. * '''1888''': Observed by archaeologist Masugoro Tsuboi, recognizing it as a kofun. * '''1914''': Detailed drawings of the chamber created by Yasunosuke Seki, Genichi Ōnishi, and others. * '''1973''': Designated as an Ise City Historical Site. * '''1975''': Excavation and cleaning of the stone chamber. == Burial Facility == The burial facility consists of a double-sleeve {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室}}, opening to the southwest. Measurements are as follows: * Total length: 18.5 meters * Main chamber: Length 9.7 meters, width 3.3 meters, height 4.1 meters * Corridor: Length 8.8 meters, width 1.9–2.6 meters, height 3.3 meters The chamber walls consist of natural stones arranged in five to six courses atop base stones. The chamber is architecturally distinctive, sharing traits with both the Kinki region and the western Mikawa region across Ise Bay. == Artifacts == Artifacts recovered during the 1975 excavation include: * Ornaments: Gold rings, tubular beads, magatama, glass beads, and cut beads. * Weapons: Swords, daggers, and iron arrowheads. * Horse fittings: Bridle parts, decorative fittings, and saddle fittings. * Pottery: Various {{ill|Sue ware|ja|須恵器}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} types. Artifacts suggest the mound was reused for additional burials into the early 7th century. == Cultural Significance == === Ise City Designated Cultural Properties === * Historical Site: Takakurayama Kofun. Designated December 5, 1973. == Notes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == References == {{small|Sources used in this article:}} * Publications by local governments: * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2005|chapter=Takakurayama Kofun|title=History of Mie Prefecture: Archaeological Materials 1|publisher=Mie Prefecture|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Mie History)|2005}}}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=2011|chapter=Special Topics > Takakurayama Kofun|title=Ise City History Volume 6: Archaeology|publisher=Ise City|ref={{Harvid|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise History)|2011}}}} == Related Literature == {{small|Additional references not directly cited in this article:}} * {{Cite book|ja|editor=|author=|year=1965|chapter=Ise City’s Takakurayama Burial Mound|title=Fubito Volume 24|publisher=Archaeological Society of Mie University|ref=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山 (伊勢市)}} * {{ill|Toyouke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} * {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Ise, Mie]] (Ise)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Qqqqqqq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:横穴式石室]][[sn:Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)]][[Category:横穴式石室]]
